item.title: Who are the Houthis? Iran's 'powerful ally' putting 'significant' pressure on Trump
item.description: The latest Houthi attack on Israel comes after years of back-and-forth attacks between the group and the US in the Red Sea.

item.title: A fuel excise cut is on its way, but is it a big 'mistake' for Australian households?
item.description: Economists warn that a fuel excise cut could boost inflation — and make mortgage rate hikes more likely.

item.title: Relief for struggling motorists as government slashes fuel tax
item.description: After weeks of pressure over surging petrol prices, the federal government has cut the tax on fuel in half. It's a three-month measure costing the budget billions, as the Prime Minister steps out a four-part plan to respond to the conflict.

item.title: Why the real crunch point on oil supply hasn’t hit Australia yet
item.description: Brent crude surges as Middle East tensions escalate, sending fresh shockwaves through global energy markets and raising concerns the real supply crunch has yet to reach Australia. In this episode of the SBS On the Money Podcast, analysis from Saul Kavonic, Head of Energy Research at MST Marquee, unpacks why this spike is sharper and faster than 2022, while Luke McMillan from Ophir Asset Management explains what it all means for the ASX after shares fell amid rising inflation fears and interest rate pressure.

item.title: 'Evil man': A timeline of the seven-month manhunt for Dezi Freeman
item.description: The search for alleged police shooter Dezi Freeman has been one of Australia's largest police operations in history.

item.title: New government inquiry could turn up the heat on Australian gas exporters
item.description: The war in the Middle East has Australian gas exporters on track for huge profits, prompting calls for an urgent 25 per cent tax on gas exports.

item.title: Full list: The solar, battery, and EV rebates and incentives you can get right now
item.description: These government rebates and incentives can help cut costs — but what you can access can depend on where you live.

item.title: A new front in the energy crisis? A 'catastrophic' threat looms over another key waterway
item.description: The Bab al-Mandab Strait, or the 'Gate of Tears', could be targeted next, threatening another critical artery for global oil flow.

item.title: Lea says she and her sister didn't talk for a year. This is why estrangements are common
item.description: Sibling estrangement is common — and can arise from rivalry or differing life stages.

item.title: Outcry as Israel blocks cardinal from Jerusalem's Holy Sepulchre on Palm Sunday
item.description: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has since reversed the ban.

item.title: Talks and threats: US signals both escalation and diplomacy in Iran
item.description: US President Donald Trump says the regime change in Iran has been achieved, as more US troops arrive in the region. But Prime Minister Anthony Albanese is urging an end point to the conflict, warning of a growing global economic damage.

item.title: US operation in Iran 'well ahead of schedule' says Secretary of State | SBS News in Easy English 31 March 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Fair Work Commission scraps discount pay rates for young Australian workers
item.description: The decision to scrap discounted pay rates applies to young-adult workers aged 18-20 in retail and fast-food jobs.

item.title: 'Inhumane': Australia criticises Israel's death penalty law targeting Palestinians
item.description: The law also technically apply to Jewish Israelis, but critics say this is extremely unlikely.

item.title: Israeli ambassador rejects plea from Zomi Frankcom’s family and refuses to apologise
item.description: Zomi Frankcom's brother will meet with the prime minister, demanding justice ahead of the second anniversary of her killing by Israel.

item.title: Human Rights Watch condemns new Israeli death penalty law
Australia investigating tech giants over teen social media ban breaches
item.description: A compliance report on the legislation found platforms were making it easy to find ways to circumvent age-assurance measures.

item.title: The life and work of Aunty Rhoda Roberts honoured on Bundjalung country
item.description: A trailblazer of Australia's arts and cultural landscape, Aunty Rhoda Roberts has been laid to rest on Country in her hometown of Lismore. Hundreds gathered to honour a life dedicated to the arts and elevating Indigenous voices.

item.title: Card surcharges set to disappear as markets end tough March on positive note
item.description: A major shake-up to how Australians pay is on the way, with the Reserve Bank moving to scrap $1.6 billion in card surcharges and shift costs into upfront pricing. In this episode of SBS On the Money, Ricardo Gonçalves breaks down what it means for households and businesses, with insights from Finder’s Graham Cooke, while Chris Brycki from Stockspot unpacks a rebound on the ASX after a volatile March that delivered the market’s worst monthly result since 2022.

item.title: 'Arriving at their doorsteps': The crisis facing Australia's neighbours
item.description: War in the Middle East is triggering supply chain disruptions that could hit the Asia Pacific's most vulnerable hardest.

item.title: What 720 Australians reveal about ageing well — and the key to longevity
item.description: Researchers have identified seven key components in ageing well.

item.title: Australia has strict gun laws but is now a hotspot for this type of gun — just like the US
item.description: Across Australia, police are encountering more homemade firearms in seizures, including some that are largely 3D-printed.

item.title: Socceroos young gun Irankunda electrifies in 5-1 thumping of Curaçao
item.description: Nestory Irankunda showed his raw potential during the Socceroos' last home game ahead of the FIFA World Cup in June.

item.title: Australia responds to Donald Trump's 'get your own oil' tirade
item.description: The United Kingdom and France were singled out by the US president for not joining the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Chalmers faces recession, fuel mandate questions amid 'significant economic shock'
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers said the quicker the war in the Middle East was resolved, the sooner the global economy could recover.

item.title: Chalmers faces recession, fuel mandate questions amid 'significant economic shock'
item.description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers said the quicker the war in the Middle East was resolved, the sooner the global economy could recover.

item.title: Albanese to address the nation about response to the war in the Middle East
item.description: The address comes amid growing concerns over a recession and the impact of fuel shortages.

item.title: Will Australia compete in Eurovision Asia 2026? SBS responds
item.description: Bangkok in Thailand will host the first Asian edition of the Eurovision Song Contest, with at least 10 countries taking part.

item.title: Donald Trump says the US attacks on Iran will end in two or three weeks | SBS News in Easy English 1 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Donald Trump says the US attacks on Iran will end in two or three weeks | SBS News in Easy English 1 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Report finds widespread discrimination against trans and gender diverse people
item.description: A new report has found discrimination against trans and gender-diverse people is widespread, with serious impacts on safety and dignity. Released to coincide with the International Transgender Day of Visibility, it makes key recommendations for governmental reform.

item.title: 'Stop using women's bodies' aid group warns, amid widespread sexual violence in Sudan
item.description: Women and girls fleeing Sudan’s war are describing rape, torture and abuse, with aid workers warning the suffering runs far deeper than the cases reaching medical care. A new report from Médecins Sans Frontières says sexual violence in Darfur is widespread, and many survivors remain out of reach.

item.title: Leading Australian Jewish group condemns Israel's death penalty laws
item.description: The new laws mean Palestinians in the West Bank convicted of killing Israelis will be sentenced to death by hanging.

item.title: 'Regime change' or not? Trump's key Iran claim under scrutiny
item.description: Donald Trump's claim of regime change has been disputed, with one expert saying he is "increasingly desperate" to frame the war as a victory.

item.title: PM urges Australians to 'do their bit' in national address that breaks convention
item.description: In a rare national address, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has advised Australians on how they can help amid fuel shortages.

item.title: ASX adds $68bn as rate rises hit Sydney and Melbourne property prices
item.description: The ASX 200 rebounds strongly after its worst month in four years, adding nearly $70 billion in value as hopes grow for an easing in global tensions, with insights from Matt Sherwood at Perpetual. At the same time, rising interest rates are beginning to weigh on the housing market, with Sydney and Melbourne recording declines even as national prices edge higher. Stephanie Youseff speaks with Tim Lawless from Cotality to unpack what’s driving the shift and whether a broader downturn is emerging, alongside the key consumer finance and market stories shaping the day.

item.title: PM calls on Australians to help each other and help the country
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has delivered his first National Address since being elected in 2022, calling on Australians to remain calm amid the ongoing fuel crisis. The Prime Minister acknowledged the impacts the war in the Middle East is having on rising petrol and diesel prices, and says farmers, truck drivers, small businesses and families are especially vulnerable to rising costs. He's urged Australians not to take more fuel than is needed, and to use public transport wherever possible to keep fuel available where it's needed most. SBS World News host Janice Petersen speaks afterwards to Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson for more on what it means.

item.title: WA Premier activates emergency powers around fuel supply
item.description: WA Premier Roger Cook says despite success in ensuring fuel is reaching the regions, efforts are being hampered by a lack of supply visibility.

item.title: The seven-word reality check in Anthony Albanese's address to the nation
item.description: The prime minister moved to reassure Australians and promised to protect the nation, but his speech wasn't without ominous undertones.

item.title: What Australia does next is built into NZ’s fuel crisis playbook
item.description: New Zealand's response to global fuel shocks has been largely similar to Australia's — with one key criticism.

item.title: I grew up the golden child before becoming the black sheep. I’m much happier now
item.description: Growing up in India, Sowmya Hiremath was the favourite child — until she rebelled. Choosing herself unexpectedly changed the family dynamic.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz tensions may linger for months after US exit from Iran
item.description: Donald Trump has indicated a quick end to the war in the Middle East is in sight, raising questions about Hormuz and a global oil shock.

item.title: INTERVIEW: PNG's Foreign Minister on his country's approach to the fuel crisis
item.description: As the fuel crisis deepens, concern is growing for the Pacific's island nations, many of who have heavy reliance on oil supplies. Questions are being asked about how Australia could help as they begin to feel the pinch of the fuel crisis. Papua New Guinea's foreign minister Justin Tkatchenko spoke to SBS Chief political correspondent Anna Henderson about PNG's supplies - but says he won't rule out receiving support from security partners if needed, including China.

item.title: What's open (and what's not) across the Easter long weekend
item.description: Here's your guide to the patchwork of closures, reduced hours and state-by-state variances across the country this Easter.

item.title: A game of cat and mouse: Russia's new target in its 'great crackdown'
item.description: From jamming mobile networks to arresting protesters, Russia's internet crackdown is accelerating.

item.title: Trump tells weary public Iran war goals nearly accomplished | SBS News in Easy English 2 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Greens accuse PM of not 'having guts' to tackle gambling following partial ad ban
item.description: The gambling reforms, three years in the making, have been labelled "laughable" by the Greens.

item.title: Extra savings on the way as Queensland and Victoria end fuel rift with states
item.description: The price drop was in doubt after Queensland and Victoria disagreed with other states on how to return extra GST revenue to consumers.

item.title: Three leaders, one shortage: How Trump, Albanese and Starmer's war addresses compared
item.description: World leaders respond to war-driven fuel shock, but diverge on relief, military action and long-term economic strategy.

item.title: Easter chocolate prices bite as markets slide after Trump's address
item.description: The ASX closes the short trading week down 1.1 per cent on Thursday as volatility returns following Donald Trump’s latest address, with insights from Morningstar’s Matt Wacher on what’s driving markets. Meanwhile, chocolate prices are leaving a bitter taste ahead of Easter, with confectionery costs up 6.6 per cent over the past year despite easing cocoa prices. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Tim Piper from the Australian Industry Group to unpack why shoppers are still paying more.

item.title: Trump’s ‘Stone Age’ threat to Iran, Cuba’s oil lifeline & a succulent Chinese meal canonised
item.description: US President Donald Trump issues a new Middle East war deadline as Yemen’s Houthis put pressure on vital waterways. Plus, the US permits a rare oil shipment to ease Cuba’s energy crisis - what’s driving the sudden pivot? And Australia immortalises an iconic ‘dinner’ felon.

item.title: Cocoa prices are at a three-year low. Why does chocolate still cost so much this Easter?
item.description: Cocoa prices fell through most of 2025, but it hasn't translated into cheaper Easter chocolate in 2026.

item.title: Not fair: Matildas and Japan join forces to call out Asian Cup pay inequality
item.description: Despite the resounding success of the 2026 Women's Asian Cup, the prize money was a pittance compared to the men's tournament.

item.title: A 'wake-up call': How can Australia realistically reduce its reliance on oil?
item.description: From electrics to biofuels, experts suggest Australia has multiple options to reduce its dependence on oil.

item.title: Trump just fired one of his key allies, Pam Bondi. Here's why
item.description: Bondi, who had faced criticism over her handling of the Epstein files, will no longer serve as US attorney general.

item.title: ‘Last of its kind’: After 175 years this church-run winery is still going strong
item.description: This Easter, Australia’s last surviving Jesuit-run winery is celebrating 175 years of continuous production. Founded by religious refugees from Europe, Sevenhill Cellars now exports wine to the world.

item.title: What Trump's 100 per cent pharmaceutical tariff means for Australia
item.description: Health Minister Mark Butler called the decision "deeply disappointing and deeply concerning".

item.title: What is Hezbollah and why is it involved in the war in the Middle East?
item.description: As fighting intensifies between Israel and Hezbollah, Lebanon is being pulled deeper into a conflict with no obvious end.

item.title: Where are Australia's EV 'charging deserts'?
item.description: High petrol prices are driving increased demand for EVs. But how easy are they to charge?

item.title: Two US military planes shot down, crew member missing, Iranian state media says
item.description: US officials confirmed a fighter jet had been shot down, but it was unclear whether the second aircraft crashed or was downed by Iran.

item.title: Daylight saving is almost over — how will it affect your time zone?
item.description: When daylight saving ends, Australia effectively loses two of its five time zones.

item.title: Bowen says fuel supply 'strong', tells Easter travellers not to cancel plans
item.description: The energy minister said fuel supplies were holding steady, with dozens of shipments en route and service station shortages declining.

item.title: Russia shifts tactics with daytime attack in 'Easter escalation'
item.description: Moscow is shifting tactics and carrying out drone and missile strikes during the daytime, launching another attack on Good Friday.

item.title: 'Peak of the iceberg': The women at the centre of a warzone's dark crisis
item.description: A new report from Médecins Sans Frontières has found sexual violence is being used a weapon of war against women in Sudan.

item.title: There were once throngs of pilgrims. Now silence fills Jerusalem's streets
item.description: Easter in Jerusalem has been stilled this year — exposing how Palestinian Christians have long practised faith under constraint.

item.title: 'Even stones tell stories': Inside the historic winery that's survived world wars and a pandemic
item.description: A historic winery preserves tradition while adapting to shifting demand, industry oversupply and an uncertain future.

item.title: Charities feeling the pressure over Easter due to rising fuel prices
item.description: Easter is a busy period for many charities offering support and relief for those in need. But the price of fuel is impacting how charities carry out their services. Some are now questioning the sustainability of those services.

item.title: Iran claims to have downed multiple US aircraft during mission to rescue soldier
item.description: Iranian authorities have said several US aircraft, including two C-130 transport planes and two Black Hawk helicopters, were destroyed.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Professor Paul Griffin tells SBS 'more important than ever' to get flu vaccine this year
item.description: 'Get vaccinated now' was an instruction we heard repeatedly during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic and since then Australia's vaccination rates have been declining. So much so that last year Australia recorded its worst flu season on record with 1,738 deaths. Director of Infectious Diseases at the Mater, Professor Paul Griffin, says everyone should get a flu vaccination. He's been telling SBS that this year's flu vaccine includes protection against a highly contagious mutation of influenza A which is known as subclade K or Super-K.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Are Australia's institutions equipped for gender diversity?
item.description: A national report has found discrimination against transgender people is still widespread across institutions, including in healthcare, housing, education and employment. The report by the Australian Human Rights Commission makes 19 recommendations, including reforms across Australian states and territories to protect gender diverse people from vilification, incitement of hatred and threats of physical harm. The author of the report, Sex Discrimination Commissioner Anna Cody, spoke to Razanne Al-Abdeli about the findings, including identifying the barriers gender-diverse people experience in different institutional settings.

item.title: The Super-K flu is spreading — it has experts worried after last year's record high flu season
item.description: Australia has experienced an unusually early start to the flu season because of the fast-spreading variant.

item.title: Economic, fuel-price shocks to be felt long after war in Middle East ends, government warns
item.description: The warning of lingering shocks comes as the NSW and WA governments announced measures to tighten monitoring of fuel retailers.

item.title: This industry faced near total collapse in Australia. How did it come back from the brink?
item.description: Australia's game industry is levelling up, with independent studios hitting high scores despite ongoing challenges.

item.title: Pope rails against war in Easter address
Iran responds to Trump's expletive-filled threat; Reports of 45-day ceasefire talks
item.description: US President Donald Trump has reissued an ultimatum to Iran, saying the US will attack its power plants and bridges on Tuesday.

item.title: Iran says World Cup pull-out increasingly likely unless matches relocated
item.description: Iran has confirmed its chances of playing in the US are "very low" as it awaits security guarantees and a decision from FIFA.

item.title: Road deaths continue to rise over Easter long weekend | SBS News in Easy English 6 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Tit-for-tat? Iran warns Trump over profane Strait of Hormuz threat
item.description: US President Donald Trump is ramping up his pressure on Iran in an expletive-laden post over the Strait of Hormuz closure. But Tehran is warning him over the threat, saying the strait will only open if transit revenues are partially set aside to compensate war damages. As strikes and military attacks continue to escalate in the region, it’s unclear if there's an end in sight to the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Human Rights Commission accepts racial discrimination complaint against NSW minister
item.description: A NSW minister and Multicultural NSW allegedly failed to address concerns raised about caste-based discrimination and Islamophobia.

item.title: Australia can rely on Asian fuel suppliers, government says | Evening News Bulletin 6 April 2026
item.description: The federal government says Australia can rely on continued fuel exports from Asia; Calls for patience as families head home from the Easter long weekend break; A heroes welcome home for Graham Arnold from the Iraqi community in Sydney.

item.title: Sprint sensation Sha'Carri Richardson sets new record in Stawell Gift
item.description: The Olympic medallist took out the historic 120m handicap race in western Victoria in 13.15 seconds.

item.title: Graham Arnold no longer coaches Australia, but he was mobbed by fans at the airport
item.description: Celebrations span continents as Iraq qualifies amid disruption, with fans gathering in Sydney to thank the coach behind it.

item.title: 'He's the best coach in the world': Ecstatic Iraqi fans greet Graham Arnold
item.description: Former Socceroos coach Graham Arnold has received a rapturous reception at Sydney Airport. Jubilant members of the Iraqi community gathering to greet him upon his return home, after he guided Iraq to their first World Cup berth in 40 years.

item.title: Australia has locked in another month's worth of fuel, but consumers and motorists brace for higher prices
item.description: The government says Australia has locked in another month's worth of fuel, as consumers and motorists brace for higher prices. With U-S President Donald Trump again lashing out at Iran, the government warns the impacts of this war could at best stretch on for months.

item.title: Bowen says fuel supply secure until May, as he warns against storing petrol at home
item.description: The energy minister's remarks came after there was a 30 per cent spike in demand for fuel over the Easter break.

item.title: 'Hurting and humiliating us': Iraqis meet the US veterans who shattered their childhoods
item.description: The Iraq War left deep scars for both Iraqi civilians and former US soldiers still coming to terms with their actions.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: 'Need to answer for itself': AG's response after Trump lashes out at NATO and Pacific allies
item.description: The attorney-general has brushed off criticism from Donald Trump, after he shared frustrations over a lack of support from Australia.

item.title: Artemis II breaks Apollo 13’s distance record as humans travel farther from Earth than ever before
item.description: Artemis II astronauts on a lunar flyby mission have broken Apollo 13's distance record as humans travel further from earth than ever before. The crew are witnessing the moon from roughly 4,000 miles above its darkened surface as it eclipses what will appear to be a basketball-sized Earth in the distant background. They're now cruising along a path in the moon's gravitational sphere of influence that will take them over the shadowed lunar far side.

item.title: Russian attack in Odesa kills three, including a toddler
item.description: Ukraine is escalating aerial attacks on Russian energy infrastructure, striking assets including ports in an effort to reduce the nation's ability to produce oil. The countries continue to trade deadly blows, as Ukraine's leader shares concerns the war in Iran is draining supplies Ukraine needs for its own defence.

item.title: Australian War Memorial alters Ben Roberts-Smith display after war crime charges
item.description: The Australian War Memorial said the display would remain on show but had been updated to reflect the allegations.

item.title: Trump says Iran can be 'taken out' for not reopening Strait of Hormuz as a ceasefire stalls
item.description: US President Donald Trump is threatening to 'decimate' Iran, if the country doesn't reopen the Strait of Hormuz. But Tehran is demanding a halt to the attacks, saying there needs to be a permanent end to hostilities in the region.

item.title: Former Sydney nanny accused of being dictator's secret agent to be extradited from Australia
item.description: Sydney woman Adriana Rivas' final appeal to avoid extradition to Chile to face historical kidnapping charges has failed.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 7 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Australian Army reservist charged with joining Ukrainian war effort granted bail
item.description: The man allegedly travelled to Ukraine in May 2025 and worked as a drone operator before returning to Australia in January 2026.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith charged with war crimes in Afghanistan
item.description: Former soldier Ben Roberts-Smith has been arrested at Sydney Airport and charged with five counts of the war crime of murder. The 47-year-old was taken into custody after arriving on a flight from Brisbane.

item.title: Mother-of-three faces deportation after decade-old visa mistake
item.description: A local South Australian community is rallying around for a woman who could be deported to Taiwan in less than a fortnight, leaving behind her husband and 3 young children.Ying-Hsi Chou is pleading with Immigration Minister Tony Burke to allow her to stay in Murray Bridge, after being told she breached her visa over a decade ago.

item.title: Israel issues fresh warning as Tehran synagogue 'completely destroyed' by US-Israeli strikes
item.description: Iranian media reported the destruction of a Tehran synagogue while the Israeli military warned Iranians to avoid taking trains.

item.title: EV sales hit a record as ASX climbs to a four month high
item.description: Listen to Australian and world news, and follow trending topics with SBS News Podcasts. TRANSCRIPT: The ASX climbs 1.7 per cent to a four-month high as strong gains sweep across all sectors, with insights from Kai Chen of MPC Markets on what is driving the rally. At the same time, rising fuel prices are helping push electric vehicle sales to new levels, with Tony Weber from the Federal Chamber of Automotive Industries unpacking whether Australia is nearing a turning point. Plus, Kim Farrant from HESTA warns super fund members about the risks of switching investment strategies during market volatility, as more Australians move towards defensive options despite recent market rebounds.

item.title: The problems quietly working against the Artemis II crew
item.description: Despite their extensive training, the Artemis II crew have encountered challenges that have troubled astronauts for six decades.

item.title: 'You need to be a breadwinner': The parenting views dividing young dads
item.description: As traditional views of masculinity regain traction among some young men, Kelvin is challenging ideas around a father's role.

item.title: Why Trump can't walk away from the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The US president's inconsistent messaging about the Strait of Hormuz signals no "clear strategy", experts say.

item.title: 'A whole civilisation will die': Trump threatens Iran once more as oil deadline looms
item.description: Iran has rejected Trump's demands and threatened to retaliate against infrastructure belonging to US allies in the Gulf.

item.title: Trump announces 'double-sided' ceasefire as Iran gives two-week Hormuz passage pledge
item.description: Iran said it would allow "safe passage" through the Strait of Hormuz, but only in coordination with its armed forces and subject to "technical limitations".

item.title: Back from the brink? Iran's 10-point plan to end war with the US
item.description: Donald Trump backed a "workable" proposal hours before a deadline for major escalation against Iran. What exactly does it include?

item.title: Strait of Hormuz to re-open under ceasefire | SBS News in Easy English 8 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Ben Roberts-Smith to remain in custody after arrest over war crimes charges
item.description: The 47-year-old is accused of the murder of unarmed civilians while deployed in Afghanistan between 2009 and 2012.

item.title: Artemis II's journey around the moon: as it happened in pictures
item.description: Here are some of the most amazing images to emerge from the first crewed lunar fly-by in more than 50 years.

item.title: Markets surge as ceasefire hopes spark $80bn rally on the ASX
item.description: Australia’s sharemarket has recorded its strongest session in about a year, with the ASX200 jumping 2.6 per cent and adding around $80 billion in value as investors responded to a reported two-week ceasefire agreement between the United States and Iran. Gains were broad-based, with eight of eleven sectors higher and technology stocks leading the charge, while gold miners and airlines also rallied amid sharp moves in commodity prices. The Australian dollar climbed to around 70.7 US cents. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Kyle Rodda, Senior Financial Market Analyst at Capital.com, to unpack what’s driving the surge and what it could mean for markets next.

item.title: Motorists' pain at the pump likely to continue for some time
item.description: The announcement of a ceasefire in the Middle East looks set to reduce motorists' pain at the pump, with Iran agreeing to allow ships through the Strait of Hormuz.But even if the ceasefire holds, it's likely price reductions will take time to flow through, and disruption to global markets could have a long tail. The government is again urging calm, saying fuel supplies are secure into May, as it works to shore up supply from international partners.

item.title: Why the 'flow-on effects' from re-opening the Strait of Hormuz could take months
item.description: The economically vital Strait of Hormuz could reopen imminently following a snap ceasefire announcement between the US and Iran.

item.title: If your stomach feels more sensitive than usual, this could be the reason
item.description: Social eating and exposure to sunlight might be linked to flare-ups of bowel symptoms.

item.title: How Australia's investigations into alleged misconduct in Afghanistan differ to the world's
item.description: Experts say Australia's response to alleged war crimes in Afghanistan has differed from some of its key allies.

item.title: South Australian mother faces deportation after passport mistake
item.description: A South Australian community is rallying around a woman who could be deported, leaving behind her husband and children.

item.title: White House suggests 10-point ceasefire plan published by Iran not what US agreed to
item.description: Iran claimed the ceasefire included Lebanon. Israel claimed it didn't. The US says there's been a misunderstanding.

item.title: 'Ketamine Queen' gets 15 years jail over the death of Matthew Perry
item.description: A warning - the following story contains elements that may distress some people. A woman dubbed the Ketamine Queen has been sentenced to 15 years in prison for selling Friends star Matthew Perry the drug that killed him in 2023. Forty-two-year-old Jasveen Sangha is the third of five people to be sentenced over Perry’s overdose.

item.title: As a 'clog wog', Australians didn't know I was a migrant, so they confided their racism
item.description: Annette Jorgensen moved here from Denmark aged two. Living as a migrant while looking like a white Australian was an insightful experience.

item.title: Wong 'not confident' over Hormuz as she calls for Israel, Hezbollah to 'respect ceasefire'
item.description: Foreign Minister Penny Wong has urged Israel to halt its attacks on Lebanon, warning the conflict threatens a "fragile" ceasefire.

item.title: Deal or no deal? What is happening with the Iran US ceasefire
item.description: Just hours after the world welcomed news of a two-week ceasefire between the U-S and Iran - which would have seen the Strait of Hormuz open up passage of much of the globe's oil supply - the fragile truce appears to be at serious risk. Iran now says it will target any ships that attempt to cross the major shipping passage - blaming deadly Israeli attacks on Lebanon overnight, which the U-S says were not subject to the ceasefire pact. So what happens now? And what have Iran and the US actually agreed to?

item.title: PM says Middle East ceasefire should include Lebanon | SBS News in Easy English 9 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Record-setting Tropical Cyclone Maila heads for flood-weary Australian region
item.description: Residents of far north Queensland are still cleaning up after Tropical Cyclone Narelle carved through the region just weeks ago.

item.title: Greece joins growing list of countries considering ban on social media for teens
item.description: Greece has joined the growing list of European countries who are proposing a social media ban for teenagers in their countries, citing concerns around anxiety and addiction. The announcement comes as Australia's teenage social media ban received mixed outcomes and feedback, after the federal government launched the world-first ban four months ago.

item.title: The ways the 'fragile' US-Iran ceasefire is already being tested
item.description: Both sides are already accusing each other of violations. These are the pressure points that could unravel the truce.

item.title: 'We cannot face him': Family of Afghan man allegedly killed by Ben Roberts-Smith speaks out
item.description: An Afghan man whose brother and father were allegedly murdered by Australian soldiers has spoken out following Ben Roberts-Smith's arrest.

item.title: US-Iran truce tested, BRS war crime charges & NASA boldly goes…
item.description: A shaky ceasefire announced between US President Donald Trump and Iran as oil prices plummet in response. Decorated veteran Ben Roberts-Smith charged with war crimes, what they could mean for Australia’s military reputation. Plus, allegations of foreign interference overshadow Hungary’s upcoming election, and around the moon and back with Artemis II.

item.title: ASX climbs as fuel habits shift and workers fall behind on AI skills
item.description: The ASX pushes higher after a strong rally, edging closer to record territory, while fresh data reveals Australians are changing how they buy fuel as prices stay elevated. NAB Senior Economist Taylor Nugent unpacks why motorists are topping up more often, and what it says about cost pressures. At the same time, new figures show fewer people are investing in work-related training, raising questions about whether employers are doing enough to prepare workers for an AI-driven future. PwC Chief People Officer Karen Lonergan weighs in on the growing skills gap, before Nick Schoenmaker from Portfolio Construction Forum breaks down the latest moves on the sharemarket and what’s driving investor confidence.

item.title: Commute-cutting proposal for Australian workers, as fuel crisis bites
item.description: Some workers are spending more than $100 extra a week in fuel costs, and there are now calls for more flexible working arrangements.

item.title: He's got Trump and Putin's praise — but are they about to lose their ally in Europe?
item.description: Hungary's election this weekend is "one of the most momentous elections in Europe and for Europe in many years," according to one analyst.

item.title: Israel agrees to Lebanon peace talks as strike deaths climb above 300
item.description: The news comes a day after Israel launched its largest wave of strikes on Lebanon since 2 March.

item.title: Israel to enter peace talks in Lebanon | Morning News Bulletin 10 April 2026
item.description: Israel agrees to talks in Lebanon after a wave of attacks against Hezbollah; Anthony Albanese to meet Singapore's leader for talks on the fuel crisis; And in the AFL, St Kilda's Lance Collard pleads not guilty to using a homophobic slur.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz traffic still a trickle despite ceasefire, no new attacks
item.description: Hundreds of ships with thousands of crew remain stranded on either side of the Strait.

item.title: Hungary goes to the polls; will Victor Orban hang on to power?
item.description: Hungary goes to the polls; will Victor Orban hang on to power? Hungarians head to the polls this weekend in an election that could have repercussions across Europe, and in Ukraine. Sitting Prime Minister Victor Orbán has been endorsed by his counterparts in Russia and the United States. Opponents say he's degraded democracy over five consecutive terms.

item.title: What Albanese's trip to Singapore could mean for Australia's fuel supply
item.description: Albanese has warned that a lasting end to the war in the Middle East would not necessarily mean it's "back to business as usual".

item.title: Russia and Ukraine agree to two-day Orthodox Easter ceasefire
item.description: "People need an Easter without threats and a real move towards peace," Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said.

item.title: Trump says Iran doing 'poor job' of letting oil through Hormuz, shaking fragile truce
item.description: Iran and the United States said the strait would reopen after the two-week truce was announced on Wednesday.

item.title: Calls for tougher peacekeeper protections as Israel and Hezbollah blamed for Indonesian soldier deaths
item.description: Italian Foreign Minister Antonio Tajani has expressed indignation after Italian peacekeepers travelling from southern Lebanon toward Beirut were blocked by warning shots fired by the Israeli military. It is one of the most recent incidents where peacekeepers have come under attack in Lebanon.

item.title: Leaders of Australia and Singapore discuss fuel security | Midday News Bulletin 10 April 2026
item.description: The leaders of Australia and Singapore discuss fuel security; the Israeli Prime Minister says Israel will continue military action in Lebanon while he pursues negotiations with Beirut; and in tennis, Alex de Minaur advances to the Monte Carlo Masters quarterfinal.

item.title: INTERVIEW - SBS speaks with UN World Food Programme Country Director Allison Oman Lawi in Beirut
item.description: SBS World News speaks with UN World Food Programme Country Director Allison Oman Lawi, who is currently in Beirut.

item.title: Global foreign aid in freefall, and Australia is low on the list
item.description: The OECD says that governments tightening their belts on foreign aid will mean hundreds of thousands of preventable deaths.

item.title: Discussions on fuel agreement between Australia and Singapore | SBS News in Easy English 10 April 2026
item.description: A daily news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Colleagues pay tribute after Queensland MP Jimmy Sullivan, 44, found dead at home
item.description: A state MP has been found dead at his home, prompting condolences to his family from all sides of politics.

item.title: No Australian navy ships going to Middle East | Evening News Bulletin 10 April 2026
item.description: Australia's acting prime minister dismisses suggestions the country lacks the capability to send warships to Iran; fallout over the arrest of former soldier Ben Roberts-Smith; and in sports, Andrew Redmayne to play his 300th A-League game in on the New South Wales Central Coast.

item.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz actually open? The possible rise of Iran's new world shipping order
item.description: The major shipping waterway has been at the heart of ceasefire discussions, with mixed reporting over its functionality.

item.title: US to host Israel-Lebanon talks amid fears for the Iran truce
item.description: Within hours of a US–Iran ceasefire, Israel launched strikes across Lebanon, killing hundreds and leaving civilians terrified, as confusion grew over whether the deal ever applied there. Meanwhile Benjamin Netanyahu says Israel will keep fighting Hezbollah but is open to talks, as negotiations in Pakistan and Washington raise hopes of diplomacy as the violence continues.

item.title: Singapore says it won't restrict fuel to Australia as the countries sign new energy deal
item.description: Australia's largest supplier of refined fuel will continue to provide petrol and diesel amid global uncertainty as part of a new agreement.

item.title: Victoria making voting more accessible for neurodivergent people
item.description: The Victorian Electoral Commission will introduce low-sensory voting at this year's state election, in a move to make elections more accessible to neurodivergent voters.

item.title: ASX has best week since October 2022 even as super returns slide
item.description: A volatile stretch for markets has taken a bite out of Australians’ super, with SuperRatings CEO Kirby Rappell explaining how recent gains have been erased amid global uncertainty and falling oil prices. The ASX edged lower on the day but closed out its strongest week since October 2022, with mixed sector performance and sharp losses in tech stocks. Oreana Private Wealth Chief Investment Officer Isaac Poole breaks down what is driving the market moves and what investors should watch next, in this episode of the SBS On the Money podcast.

item.title: A warning from the US ahead of ceasefire talks in Pakistan | Morning News Bulletin 11 April 2026
item.description: A warning from the US ahead of ceasefire talks in Pakistan; Singapore rules out restricting fuel exports to Australia; And in tennis, Australia's hopes of winning a place in the Billie Jean King Cup Finals off to a poor start.

item.title: Why experts seriously doubt the US' claim of 'overwhelming victory' in the war against Iran
item.description: "Everyone loses in this war," one foreign policy analyst told SBS News.

item.title: Extreme conditions and a faulty space toilet. How Artemis II returned with Australia's help
item.description: The mission faced engineering and human health challenges. Overcoming these obstacles in deep space was the whole point.

item.title: They were cleaning their homes in hope. By morning, the bombs had fallen
item.description: Strikes in Lebanon continue despite a ceasefire, deepening uncertainty and the human cost of a war it did not choose.

item.title: INTERVIEW: The Cocos Islands will soon become uninhabitable; but what happens to the residents?
item.description: A remote Australian outpost more than 3,000 kilometres from Perth, the Cocos (Keeling) Islands are facing an uncertain future. Climate modelling predicts the low-lying atoll, home to around 600 residents, could become uninhabitable within 50 years. About 460 of those residents are Cocos Malays, whose ancestors were brought to the islands generations ago as indentured labourers by the Clunies-Ross family, paid in company-issued tokens. Despite that history, the community has preserved a distinct culture and way of life that endures today. Following a United Nations-supervised vote in the 1980s, the Cocos Malays chose to integrate with Australia. Since that time, it is the isolation that has helped preserve the islands unique cultural identity. In this extended edition of Weekend One on One, Federal Minister for Local Government and Territories Kristy McBain speaks with SBS’s Christopher Tan, following the release of the Government’s Coastal Hazard Risk Management and Adaptation Plan — the final report assessing the threats facing the Cocos Islands.

item.title: Digital detox: welcome to the offline club for those wanting a screen-free connection
item.description: A new kind of club has emerged in Amsterdam - one where participants choose to go offline, away from their phones and other devices. The gathering reflects a wider trend, especially among younger people, to step away from screens and connect with others and themselves.

item.title: Iran and US still at odds on truce terms as negotiating teams arrive in Islamabad
item.description: Iran says that to end the war in the Middle East, its assets must be unblocked and a ceasefire implemented in Lebanon.

item.title: Artemis II astronauts emerge from capsule after safely returning to Earth
item.description: The Orion capsule splashed down off the coast of California after a historic 10-day mission around the moon and back.

item.title: Artemis II crew return safely after historic lunar flyby | Midday News Bulletin 11 April 2026
item.description: Artemis II crew return safely after historic lunar flyby and 10 days in space; Australia and Singapore guarantee fuel and LNG supply continuity; And in sport, a record and an upset at the Australian Athletics Championship in Sydney.

item.title: 'Really valuable': How amateur astronomers made a major discovery 'in the wrong place'
item.description: Amateur astronomers have been recognised for their discoveries that have "guided humanity’s big telescopes."

item.title: 'Splashdown confirmed' as Artemis II astronauts return to Earth from record-breaking trip to the moon
item.description: The crew of NASA's Artemis II mission have returned to Earth after creating history on their pass behind the moon. They splashed down in the Pacific this morning, after flying deeper into space than any humans before them.

item.title: Why Pakistan is hosting the highest-level US-Iran talks in almost half a century
item.description: Officials from the US and Iran are set to meet in Islamabad, with Pakistan hoping to broker an end to the war in the Middle East.

item.title: US-Iran talks set to begin in Pakistan | Evening News Bulletin 11 April 2026
item.description: US-Iran talks set to begin in Pakistan; Energy minister provides update on fuel supplies; And in sport, Supercars cancels final race of the Taupo round due to looming cyclone in New Zealand.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: Some Australians forced to choose 'between food and medicine', new survey finds
item.description: A new survey has shed light on the financial decisions faced by some Australians prescribed medicines not covered by the PBS.

item.title: 'A simpler way of being': The rise of the offline club
item.description: There's a growing movement of young people coming together to switch off their phones and rediscover life offline.

item.title: 'Beaten' and 'subject to mock executions': How protesters are being forgotten in war
item.description: Several Iranians have been executed in recent weeks in Iran; some say the regime is sending the "most horrific warning signal".

item.title: The US and Iran hold direct talks aimed at ending weeks of conflict | Morning News Bulletin 12 April 2026
item.description: The US and Iran hold direct talks, aimed at ending weeks of conflict; New shipments see Australian fuel reserves lift by two days, to 31-day supply; And in surfing, Australia's world champion Molly Picklum has fallen short in the final against Gabriela Bryan.

item.title: No deal: JD Vance leaves Pakistan after US and Iran fail to reach peace agreement
item.description: Foreign Minister Penny Wong said it was "disappointing" that a deal had not been reached, urging the US and Iran to return to negotiations.

item.title: Darwin multicultural walking group connecting migrants one step at a time
item.description: A local program in Darwin is helping refugees and new migrants connect with their community, while building life-long friendships. The walking group may be simple, but organisers say at a time when more people are facing conflict and displacement, the initiative provides a welcome escape.

item.title: Human rights groups warn about systematic persecution of Baha'i followers
item.description: Executions have continued despite war in Iran, with human rights organisations warning the regime is sending a brutal message to citizens who challenge it.Fears are mounting in the minority Baha'i community, with reports some members have been subjected to extreme torture in recent weeks. This story was produced in collaboration with SBS Persian and includes disturbing concepts.

item.title: Why Melbourne woman Ildi wants to see the end of Viktor Orbán's time ruling over Hungary
item.description: From fencers to shopkeepers, Hungarians weigh in on an election that could end Viktor Orbán's 16-year rule.

item.title: 'Special thing to be a human': Artemis II crew welcomed home after moon mission
item.description: The four astronauts took centre stage on Saturday, a day after returning from their historic lunar flyby.

item.title: More Australians are self-prescribing natural remedies, but are they always safe?
item.description: Widely used in cold and flu supplements, andrographis has been linked to life-threatening allergic reactions.

item.title: Government considering further economic relief as motorists urged to reduce fuel use
item.description: Transport Minister Catherine King has warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz opens soon, there's "a long tail" to the fuel crisis.

item.title: Israel and Lebanon to host talks in the US as ceasefire divisions emerge | Midday News Bulletin 12 April 2026
item.description: Israel and Lebanon to host talks in the US this week, with Benjamin Netanyahu saying he wants peace; Singapore's energy minister praises bilateral relationship, after new deal with Australia; And in sailing, Australia leads the SailG-P in Rio after day one.

item.title: Better than Bolt: Gout Gout smashes 20-second barrier in 200m sprint, beats own record
item.description: The 18-year-old has become the first Australian to legally break the 20-second barrier over 200m after he recorded a time of 19.67.

item.title: US and Iran conclude talks in Pakistan without reaching a deal | Evening News Bulletin 12 April 2026
item.description: US and Iran conclude talks in Pakistan without reaching a deal; Government considering extra cost-relief measures in upcoming budget; And in athletics, Lachlan Kennedy pulls out of 200m after winning first national 100m title.

item.title: Iran's foreign ministry says diplomacy must continue after no peace deal reached
Yu went by a different name to seem more Australian, but it backfired
item.description: More than half of Australia's residents or their parents were born overseas, but many still find having a non-English-sounding name hard.

item.title: Inside Australia's push to build a sovereign missile industry
item.description: As Australia ramps up missile production, experts say supply chain gaps and export challenges could limit its independence.

item.title: PM 'disappointed' US-Iran talks failed, says Australia not asked to block Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran has restricted traffic through the strait while allowing vessels deemed to be working for friendly countries to pass.

item.title: 'Europe's heart is beating stronger': Hungary's Orbán concedes landmark defeat
item.description: European Union leader Ursula von der Leyen hailed the landslide victory by the Hungarian opposition as a move towards Europe.

item.title: US will blockade Hormuz says Trump | Morning News Bulletin 13 April 2026
item.description: Donald Trump says the US to blockade the Strait of Hormuz, as Iran says Mr Trump's threats have no impact on Iran; Viktor Orban concedes defeat in Hungary's election; and in athletics, Gout Gout sets a new 200-metre record.

item.title: India grieves as legendary Bollywood singer Asha Bhosle dies age 92
item.description: Asha Bhosle, who recorded more than 12,000 songs in multiple Indian languages over a seven‑decade career, has died in Mumbai.

item.title: 'Selfless, courageous' heroes of Bondi Junction stabbing honoured
item.description: Two years after a mass stabbing at a Sydney shopping centre, eight individuals have been recognised for their bravery.

item.title: 'A dangerous precedent': Iran wants to impose tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. Can it?
item.description: Charging ongoing fees for transit through the Strait of Hormuz would likely trigger international uproar.

item.title: What your dreams could be telling you about your Alzheimer's risk
item.description: Frequent nightmares, or the inability to recall your dreams at all, could be early signs of Alzheimer's disease.

item.title: 'Love always triumphs': Peter Magyar's Tisza party ousts VIktor Orban after 16 years
item.description: Hungary's veteran nationalist leader Viktor Orban has conceded defeat after a landslide election victory by the upstart opposition Tisza party. An end to Mr Orban's period in government after 16 years in power would have significant implications not only for Hungary, but for the European Union, Ukraine and beyond.

item.title: Europe welcomes Hungary's new Government | Midday News Bulletin 13 April 2026
item.description: Australia rules out sending ships to help in any US blockade of the Strait of Hormuz; Europe reacts to the ousting of Viktor Orban in Hungary after 16 years; Belgian Wout van Aert wins the Paris-Roubaix title.

item.title: European leaders - and voters in Hungary - welcome the defeat of Viktor Orban after 14 years in power
India begins count of its 1.4 billion people
item.description: India is about to conduct the world’s largest census of its predicted 1.4 billion population after a five year COVID-initiated delay, budgeted for almost two billion Australian dollars.More than 3 million paid surveyors, including school teachers and government officials, will head out and knock on each door in India’s hundreds of thousands villages and cities, as the task unfolds with questions around process, data security and the government claim that caste will be included for the first time since the 1930s.

item.title: Donald Trump calls Pope Leo 'weak' after pontiff's criticism of war and immigration policies
item.description: The United States president told reporters that he is "not a big fan" of Pope Leo XIV.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy on plans for a missile industry
item.description: Australia is stepping into a new phase of its military evolution, producing a type of high-precision, sniper-like missile on home soil for the first time. Along with the United States, it is now one of the only nations manufacturing these guided weapons. Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy tells Cameron Carr why the federal government believes a sovereign missile industry is so important.

item.title: Who is Péter Magyar, Hungary's new prime minister?
item.description: Railing against the Orbán regime helped his popularity explode. Now, he may be a problem for Trump and Putin.

item.title: United States to blockade Iran's ports
item.description: Marathon talks between the United States and Iran have failed to reach a deal to end the war, jeopardising a fragile two-week ceasefire. The collapse of peace negotiations, and U-S plans to blockade the Strait of Hormuz, have rattled an already-tense region and raised fears of renewed fighting.

item.title: Bondi stabbing heroes honoured with bravery awards | SBS News in Easy English 13 April 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Australia won't join Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade as deadline looms | Evening News Bulletin 13 April 2026
item.description: A U-S deadline looms for a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz as Australia declines to join in; Australia appoints the first female army chief in history; Rory McIlroy takes out the Augusta masters for a second year running.

item.title: Blockading the Strait: How Donald Trump's latest strategy could cost Australians
item.description: The US leader has threatened to escalate the war on Iran with a naval blockade that experts warn could be economically disastrous.

item.title: Markets slip as oil surges and investors ask can AI interpret geopolitical risk?
item.description: The ASX edges lower as oil prices climb above US$100 amid escalating Middle East tensions, raising fresh concerns about inflation and growth. Armina Rosenberg from Minotaur Capital breaks down whether AI-driven investing can make sense of geopolitical shocks, while Deloitte Access Economics partner David Rumbens warns Australia could be edging closer to recession, even if the conflict eases. With markets still near record highs but volatility building, this episode unpacks the risks shaping the economic outlook and what it means for investors and households.

item.title: Eight people recognised for Bondi bravery
item.description: Eight people have been recognised for their bravery during the Bondi Junction shopping centre attack. Governor-General Sam Mostyn announced the special honours to mark the two-year anniversary.

item.title: Viktor Orbán has been defeated. What does it mean for the rest of the world?
item.description: Viktor Orbán had such an outsize global influence that his crushing electoral defeat is resounding far and wide.
